items.q: In a standard hydrogen electrode used as a reference, what is the assigned electrode potential?
items.correct: Exactly 0 V by convention — all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.
items.misconception: The standard hydrogen electrode actually has zero potential because hydrogen has no charge.
items.random_wrong: The standard hydrogen electrode has a potential of -1 V to balance copper at +1 V.
items.why: Students don't realise 0 V is a chosen reference, not a measured value; they think hydrogen is genuinely neutral electrochemically.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: What is the role of the fingerprint region (below ~1500 cm^-1) in IR spectroscopy?
items.correct: It is unique to each compound — used to identify a specific molecule by matching to a known spectrum.
items.misconception: The fingerprint region is used to identify which functional groups are present.
items.random_wrong: The fingerprint region is the part that contains all the most useful absorptions.
items.why: Students reverse functional group region (above ~1500, identifies bond types) and fingerprint region (below, identifies the molecule as a whole) — both are useful, but for different purposes.

items.q: Rutherford's gold foil experiment was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1911. “1897” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mass spectrometer development is associated with:
items.why: It's Aston. “Bohr” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Rutherford proposed the nuclear atom?
items.why: Exact answer: 1911 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
